macro_segments.title: The Case of Adding Oil to the Dry Minchat Chotei and the Debate Between Rabbi Yochanan and Resh Lakish

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah on Mixed Menachot and the Positions of Chachamim and Rabbi Yehuda
The Case of Kometz from Minchat Chotei with Added Oil – Rabbi Yochanan's Position (Pasul)
Resh Lakish's Position: Adding a Little Oil from Sheiyarei HaLog is Permitted
Tosafot's Discussion: Lo Nitnah Torah L'Malachei HaShareis
The Gemara's Challenge: The Explicit Pasuk Forbids Oil on Minchat Chotei
Resolution: Lo Yikba – Don't Make It a Mixture Like Normal Menachot
Rabbi Yochanan's Challenge to Resh Lakish: The Braita About Charev Mixed with Balul
The Gemara's Reinterpretation: Two Types of Menachot Nesachim with Different Proportions
Rav Raba's Question: Kometz Whose Oil Was Squeezed onto Wood
Ravina to Rav Ashi: Connection to Rabbi Yochanan and Resh Lakish Debate on Attached Items
The Gemara's Analysis: Whether Oil (Like Bone) Must Be Considered Part of What Goes Up
The Teiku: Unresolved Whether Oil Must Go Up on the Altar

macro_segments.title: Two Unmeasured Menachot That Mixed Together – When Both Remain Kosher

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Two Menachot Not Yet Measured, Partially Mixed – If You Can Measure Each Separately, Both Are Kosher
The Mishnah: If You Cannot Measure Each Separately, Both Are Pasul
Case 2: A Kometz Gets Mixed into an Unmeasured Mincha – Do Not Offer It
If Offered Despite the Prohibition: The One Measured is Kosher, the Unmeasured is Not
Case 3: A Kometz Falls Back Into Its Own Remainder or Into Another Mincha's Remainder
If Offered Despite Prohibition: Kometz and Its Shirayim Together Make It Kosher

macro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's Principle: When Bittul Works and When It Does Not Based on Potential Status Change

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda: Neveilah Is Batel to Shechutah Because Shechutah Cannot Become Neveilah
Rav Chisda Continued: Shechutah Is Not Batel to Neveilah Because Neveilah Can Spoil and Lose Its Status
Introducing the Framework: Rabbi Yehuda's Min B'Mino Lo Batel Requires Same Physical Object and Halakhic Status
Rav Chisda's Application: Whether the Mevatel (Majority) Can Become Like the Batel (Minority)
Rabbi Chanina's Alternative: Whether the Batel (Minority) Can Become Like the Mevatel (Majority)
The Gemara's Analysis: Determining Which Position Rav Chisda and Rabbi Chanina Are Interpreting
Clarification: Rav Chisda and Rabbi Chanina Are Narrowing Rabbi Yehuda's Definition of Min B'Mino

macro_segments.title: Applying the Rav Chisda / Rabbi Chanina Debate to the Mishnah's Cases of Mixed Menachot

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Question: Why Doesn't Shirayim Do Bittul to the AB Mixture in the First Mishnah Case?
The Gemara's Answer: Rabbi Zeira's Special Rule – Kometz and Shirayim Never Do Bittul to Each Other
The Derasha for Rabbi Zeira's Rule: Same Verb (Haktarah) Used for Kometz and Shirayim Obligation
Applying Rabbi Zeira to the Second Case: Kometz Mixed with Unmeasured Mincha Should Also Be Exempt from Bittul
Testing Whether This Works According to Rav Chisda or Rabbi Chanina's Frameworks
Extending Rabbi Zeira's Principle to the Third Case: Kometz in Its Own Shirayim

macro_segments.title: Testing the Status-Based Framework Against the Case of Spiced Matza

micro_segments.title: The Baraita: Matza Spiced with Cumin or Sesame Is Still Considered Kosher Matza
The Challenge: If Spices Outnumber Matza, Why Doesn't Bittul Occur?
Testing Against Rav Chisda's Framework (Looking at Mevatel)
Testing Against Rabbi Chanina's Framework (Looking at Batel)
The Gemara's Resolution: The Case Assumes Matza Is the Majority, Not the Spices
Conclusion: This Case Does Not Settle the Rav Chisda / Rabbi Chanina Debate

topical_tags.term: Ein Olim Mevatlin Zeh Et Zeh
Lo Yikba (Do Not Make a Mixture)
Rabbi Zeira's Exception – Kometz and Shirayim
